narrative_blocks.body: Helen Mary Hooker was born in Poughkeepsie on June 12, 1819 and died on January 24, 1838 at age eighteen — before her father's career as Canal Commissioner, before her older sister Catherine married Colonel Hamersley, and before the entire arc of the Gilded Age that her sister's family would inhabit. She left no descendants, while her sister Catherine's line extended through the J. Hooker Hamersley family into the 1970s, the Hooker name surviving through the Hamersley branch's middle names long after Helen Mary's early death in Poughkeepsie.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.title: Judge
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of James Hooker (1792-1858), Surrogate of Dutchess County NY.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College Class of 1810. One of many Hooker-line Yale graduates spanning generations.
Judge — already wired in earlier stage.

education.notes: Admitted to bar in Poughkeepsie 1813 after Litchfield.

career.notes: Admitted to the bar in Poughkeepsie 1813.

career.organization: New York State Erie Canal Commission
career.notes: Appointed Canal Commissioner of New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter's line became Gilded Age Manhattan royalty
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker's daughter Catherine Livingston Hooker married Colonel John William Hamersley in 1840 — a Manhattan lawyer, traveler, and social figure who had met Muhammad Ali Pasha in Egypt and helped save the Mexican Republic from Napoleon — and their line grew into one of Gilded Age New York's most prominent families: the Colonel's son J. Hooker Hamersley published poetry, once courted Edith Roosevelt before she chose Theodore, and whose son Louis Gordon Hamersley was born to international headlines as the 'seven million dollar baby' in 1892. The Hooker who had been a Windsor merchant's son and Poughkeepsie probate lawyer thus became, within three generations, the progenitor of a Fifth Avenue dynasty that hosted a President's Cup speedboat ceremony and twelve hundred crippled veterans on a Hudson River estate.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve years as Surrogate of Dutchess County
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker served as Surrogate of Dutchess County for twelve years from 1828 to 1840 — the probate judge responsible for wills, estates, and guardianships across one of New York's most prosperous Hudson Valley counties — and was selected as Presidential Elector of New York in 1836, before being appointed Canal Commissioner of New York in 1842, overseeing operations of the Erie Canal that had transformed the American interior. He had come to Poughkeepsie in 1813 after graduating Yale (1810) and studying at Litchfield Law School, the first law school in the United States, and spent his entire career in Dutchess County, dying there in September 1858.

narrative_blocks.header: Vestryman of Christ Church for forty-one years
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker served as Vestryman of Christ Church in Poughkeepsie from 1817 — the year after he married Helen Sarah Reade and settled in Dutchess County — until his death in 1858, forty-one consecutive years in the same Episcopal congregation through the entire arc of his career as Surrogate, Canal Commissioner, and retired counsellor. This was a quiet denominational shift: the Hookers were descendants of the Puritan Rev. Thomas Hooker, a Congregationalist founder of Hartford, yet James spent his adult life in the Episcopal church — a shift that would be echoed when his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley was eulogized at Grace Church on Fifth Avenue, the grandest Episcopal church in Manhattan.

narrative_blocks.header: He married into the Reade Street Livingstons
narrative_blocks.body: On January 24, 1816, James Hooker married Helen Sarah Reade in Poughkeepsie — the granddaughter of John Reade (1745-1808) and Catherine Livingston Reade, connecting the Windsor Hooker family to both the Livingston Manor dynasty and to the colonial New York merchant family whose ancestor Joseph Reade had served as warden of Trinity Church for fifty years, sat on the Governor's Provincial Council, and given Reade Street in Lower Manhattan its name. The marriage brought into the Hooker line a thread of colonial New York aristocracy that would eventually surface in his grandson J. Hooker Hamersley — a man who traced his ancestry to both Robert Livingston the Elder (grantee of Livingston Manor by royal charter) and the Reade family whose name still runs through Lower Manhattan.

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Helen Reade Hooker (1790-1879); wife of Judge James Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Her family's name appears on a Manhattan street
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Helen Reade was the granddaughter of John Reade (1745-1808) and Catherine Livingston Reade — a member of the great Livingston family of the Hudson Valley — and her great-grandfather was Joseph Reade (1694-1771), the New York merchant and provincial councillor whose family gave Reade Street in Lower Manhattan its name, serving as warden of Trinity Church for over fifty years and counting among his in-laws the Mayor of New York City (1702-1703) and Frederick Philipse, one of the wealthiest Dutch merchants in colonial New York. She was also, through her Livingston grandmother, a fifth-generation descendant of Robert Livingston the Elder, the Scottish immigrant granted Livingston Manor by royal charter — a lineage her grandson James Hooker Hamersley would proudly enumerate in his biography.

narrative_blocks.header: Her great-grandfather advised delaying the Stamp Act
narrative_blocks.body: Joseph Reade, Sarah's great-grandfather, sat on the New York Governor's Provincial Council from 1761 until his death in 1771, and — while serving under Governor Sir Henry Moore — advised the administration to delay the issuance of stamped paper required by the Stamp Act, positioning himself among the colonial establishment figures who helped defuse that crisis before revolution became inevitable. He also sat on the grand jury of the New York Slave Conspiracy trials of 1741, which based on questionable testimony resulted in death sentences for thirty-four defendants and the deportation of ninety-one others — a reminder that his distinguished family history also carried moral complexity at its center.

sources.label: Americana Aristocracy — Helen Sarah Reade Hooker

Father-in-law and partner in the Hooker and Chaffee firm.
his partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee
brother of his wife Mary Chaffee

narrative_blocks.header: Hooker and Chaffee supplied the Continental Army
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker and Chaffee was one of New England's largest mercantile houses before the Revolution, transacting much business for the Colonial Government. The partners gave freely to the patriot cause and were seriously embarrassed at war's end.

narrative_blocks.header: His firm provisioned alongside Wadsworth's commissary
narrative_blocks.body: The Hooker and Chaffee firm operated alongside Jeremiah Wadsworth's larger commissary operation — both Hooker-Talcott descendants provisioning the same Continental Army. The Connecticut River Valley supply network was effectively a Hooker-tree internal collaboration.

narrative_blocks.header: His Windsor house is on the National Register
narrative_blocks.body: He built the James Hooker House ca. 1772 at 118 Palisado Avenue, Windsor — now part of the Palisado Avenue Historic District on the National Register of Historic Places. The interior was later gutted for Chaffee School classrooms but the Federal-period exterior survives under Greek Revival additions.

institutions.institution_blurb: Silent partner; one of New England's great Revolutionary-era trading houses.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He built this house c.1772; it survives on Palisado Avenue's National Register district.

bio.title: Captain
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Captain James Hooker (1742-1805), Windsor CT merchant.

career.notes: Principal of INST070 Hooker and Chaffee. Succeeded to father's Hartford business with brothers; established Windsor branch. See INST070 for full firm history.

parents.mother_research_notes: Lydia (Griswold) Chaffee, b.Dec 17, 1722 Simsbury CT; d.Oct 1, 1801 Windsor CT. Daughter of the Griswold family of Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Brother John was partner in Hooker & Chaffee
narrative_blocks.body: Her older brother John Chaffee was a partner in the firm Hooker & Chaffee — the business linking her birth family directly to her husband's. The 1784 marriage to Captain James Hooker thus joined both personal and commercial lines of the Windsor Connecticut Chaffee household.

She survived her husband Captain James Hooker by forty-one years — he died December 1805, she lived until October 1846 at eighty-six. After James's death she returned from Rochester New York to Windsor Connecticut and lived with her unmarried sister Hepsibah Chaffee until Hepsibah's 1834 death.
